name: performance
context: PostgreSQL performance tuning: EXPLAIN ANALYZE, indexes, connection pooling, vacuum, configuration
info: PostgreSQL performance tuning covers query analysis with EXPLAIN ANALYZE, index strategy selection (B-tree, GIN, GiST, BRIN, partial, covering), connection pooling with PgBouncer, VACUUM and autovacuum configuration for dead tuple cleanup, and server configuration tuning (shared_buffers, work_mem, effective_cache_size, maintenance_work_mem). Performance issues typically stem from missing indexes, sequential scans on large tables, excessive connections, bloated tables needing VACUUM, or misconfigured memory settings. Always start with EXPLAIN ANALYZE to identify the bottleneck before making changes.
body:
# PostgreSQL Performance Tuning

Performance tuning starts with measurement. Always use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to understand what the query planner is doing before making changes. The most common fixes are adding indexes, tuning connection pooling, running VACUUM, and adjusting memory configuration.

## EXPLAIN ANALYZE

EXPLAIN ANALYZE runs the query and shows the actual execution plan with timing:

```sql
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)
SELECT u.name, COUNT(o.id) AS order_count
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id
WHERE u.created_at > '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY u.name
ORDER BY order_count DESC
LIMIT 10;
```

Key things to look for in the output:

| Indicator | Problem | Solution |
|-----------|---------|----------|
| Seq Scan on large table | Missing index | Add index on filter/join columns |
| Nested Loop with high row count | Inefficient join | Check join columns are indexed |
| Sort with high cost | No index for ORDER BY | Add index matching sort order |
| Rows Removed by Filter (high) | Index not selective enough | Use partial index or composite index |
| Buffers: shared read (high) | Data not in cache | Increase shared_buffers or effective_cache_size |

```sql
-- Compare estimated vs actual rows
EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT * FROM users WHERE status = 'active';
-- If estimated rows differ wildly from actual, run ANALYZE:
ANALYZE users;
```

## Index Strategies

Choose the right index type for the workload:

```sql
-- B-tree (default): equality and range queries
CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users (email);
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_created ON orders (created_at DESC);

-- Composite index: multi-column lookups
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user_status ON orders (user_id, status);

-- Partial index: only index relevant rows
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_active ON orders (created_at)
WHERE status = 'active';

-- Covering index: include extra columns to enable index-only scans
CREATE INDEX idx_users_email_name ON users (email) INCLUDE (name);

-- GIN index: full-text search, arrays, JSONB
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_search ON articles USING GIN (search_vector);
CREATE INDEX idx_data_jsonb ON data USING GIN (metadata jsonb_path_ops);

-- GiST index: geospatial (PostGIS), range types
CREATE INDEX idx_locations_coords ON locations USING GIST (coords);

-- BRIN index: large append-only tables with natural ordering
CREATE INDEX idx_logs_created ON logs USING BRIN (created_at);
```

| Index Type | Best For | Size |
|------------|----------|------|
| B-tree | Equality, range, sorting | Medium |
| GIN | Full-text, arrays, JSONB, trigrams | Large |
| GiST | Geospatial, range types, nearest-neighbor | Medium |
| HNSW | Vector similarity (pgvector) | Large |
| BRIN | Append-only tables, time-series | Tiny |

## Connection Pooling

PostgreSQL creates a process per connection. Too many connections waste memory and cause contention. Use PgBouncer as a connection pooler:

```ini
# pgbouncer.ini
[databases]
mydb = host=localhost port=5432 dbname=mydb

[pgbouncer]
listen_port = 6432
listen_addr = 0.0.0.0
auth_type = md5
auth_file = /etc/pgbouncer/userlist.txt
pool_mode = transaction
max_client_conn = 1000
default_pool_size = 20
min_pool_size = 5
reserve_pool_size = 5
```

Pool modes:
- **transaction** (recommended) — connection returned to pool after each transaction
- **session** — connection held for the entire client session
- **statement** — connection returned after each statement (no multi-statement transactions)

Application-side pool sizing:
```javascript
// pg Pool configuration
const pool = new pg.Pool({
  connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL,
  max: 20,            // max connections in pool
  idleTimeoutMillis: 30000,
  connectionTimeoutMillis: 2000,
});
```

## VACUUM and Autovacuum

PostgreSQL uses MVCC — updates and deletes create dead tuples. VACUUM reclaims space:

```sql
-- Manual vacuum
VACUUM (VERBOSE) tablename;

-- Full vacuum (rewrites table, requires exclusive lock)
VACUUM FULL tablename;

-- Analyze updates statistics for the query planner
ANALYZE tablename;

-- Combined
VACUUM ANALYZE tablename;

-- Check dead tuples
SELECT schemaname, relname, n_dead_tup, n_live_tup,
       ROUND(n_dead_tup::numeric / NULLIF(n_live_tup, 0) * 100, 2) AS dead_pct
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC;
```

Autovacuum configuration in `postgresql.conf`:
```
autovacuum = on
autovacuum_vacuum_threshold = 50
autovacuum_analyze_threshold = 50
autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.1
autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor = 0.05
autovacuum_max_workers = 3
```

## Configuration Tuning

Key `postgresql.conf` settings (adjust for your server's RAM):

| Setting | Guideline | Purpose |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| shared_buffers | 25% of RAM | PostgreSQL shared memory cache |
| effective_cache_size | 50-75% of RAM | Planner's estimate of OS cache |
| work_mem | 4-64 MB | Per-operation sort/hash memory |
| maintenance_work_mem | 256 MB - 1 GB | VACUUM, CREATE INDEX memory |
| wal_buffers | 64 MB | WAL write buffer |
| max_connections | 100-300 | Use PgBouncer to multiplex |
| random_page_cost | 1.1 (SSD) / 4.0 (HDD) | Planner cost for random I/O |

```sql
-- Check current settings
SHOW shared_buffers;
SHOW work_mem;

-- Change at session level
SET work_mem = '64MB';

-- Change permanently (requires restart for some)
ALTER SYSTEM SET shared_buffers = '4GB';
SELECT pg_reload_conf();  -- for settings that don't require restart
```

sections.title: EXPLAIN ANALYZE
sections.body: Use `EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)` to see actual execution plans with timing and buffer usage. Look for Seq Scans on large tables (add index), high row estimates vs actual (run ANALYZE), and Nested Loops with many iterations (check join indexes). Always measure before optimizing. See [[postgres/queries]] for the queries to analyze.

sections.title: Index strategies
sections.body: B-tree for equality/range (default). GIN for full-text, arrays, JSONB, trigrams (see [[postgres/extensions#pg-trgm]]). GiST for geospatial and range types (see [[postgres/extensions#postgis]]). HNSW/IVFFlat for vectors (see [[postgres/extensions#pgvector]]). BRIN for time-series. Use partial indexes to index only relevant rows. Use covering indexes (`INCLUDE`) for index-only scans. Composite indexes should order columns by selectivity.

sections.title: Connection pooling
sections.body: PgBouncer is the standard PostgreSQL connection pooler. Use `transaction` mode for most applications. Set `default_pool_size` to match your database's `max_connections` divided by the number of application instances. Application-side pool (pg Pool) manages local connections; PgBouncer manages server-side. For [[postgres/queries#transactions]], transaction pool mode works correctly; session mode is needed for session-level settings like prepared statements.

sections.title: VACUUM and autovacuum
sections.body: VACUUM reclaims dead tuples from MVCC. Autovacuum runs automatically based on thresholds. Monitor `n_dead_tup` in `pg_stat_user_tables`. VACUUM FULL rewrites the table (exclusive lock, use sparingly). For high-update tables, lower `autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor` to trigger more frequent vacuuming. Run `ANALYZE` after bulk loads to update planner statistics.

sections.title: Configuration tuning
sections.body: Set `shared_buffers` to 25% of RAM, `effective_cache_size` to 50-75% of RAM. Increase `work_mem` for complex sorts and hashes (but it's per-operation, not per-connection). Set `random_page_cost = 1.1` for SSDs. Use `ALTER SYSTEM SET` for persistent changes. Some settings require restart (shared_buffers), others just reload (`SELECT pg_reload_conf()`). Use [[postgres/replication]] WAL settings for replication workloads.

links.context: HNSW and IVFFlat index tuning for vectors
GIN index tuning for trigram search
